title: The Macro Minute: Are markets adequately pricing in regime change at the Fed?

Happy Wednesday out there team 42 your
skipper here Darius Dell to present our
macro minute for Wednesday July 16th
2025. Hope everyone's having a great
week. So as always we start with the
executive summary from today's leadoff
morning note. If you would like to
review the analysis supporting these
conclusions and to see if you need to
make any changes in your portfolio to
remain on the right side of market risk
or to get on the right side of market
risk to the extent you haven't been
following our kiss of Dr. Bose signals,
you need to be a client of 42 macro. So
today's Kumacro question is are markets
adequately pricing in regime change at
the Fed. Our answer no. Kevin Hasset the
current director of the National
Economic Council is the early frontr
runner to replace Jerome Pow as Federal
Reserve chair in 2025 for sources
familiar with the process. Kevin Walsh,
the former Fed governor whom President
Trump passed over in 2017, citing
hawkish policy views and a youthful
appearance is also a top the short list
to replace pal. Sources say Treasury
Secretary Scott Bessant is helping vet
Fed chair candidates, but may land the
role himself if others fail to impress.
Fed Governor Chris Waller is also a
darkhorse candidate. Unlike former
national economic director Gary Conn,
who tried tempering Trump's tariff
instincts, Hassid has taken a maximalist
approach at the National Economic
Council. He has embraced full MAGA
orthodoxy, echoing Trump's hardline
stances on trade, taxes, and the Fed in
repeated media appearances. This is
consistent with our view that President
Trump prefers to surround himself with
loyal sick fans. For our June 16th
leadoff morning note, there's been a lot
of good news on the inflation front in
recent months. If Pal and his colleagues
fail to adequately incorporate positive
inflation developments via signaling an
incrementally dovish positive policy
outlook, both in terms of the timing and
magnitude of rate cuts, we are likely to
see President Trump move quickly to name
J. Pal's eventual replacement. Someone
our analysis suggests is likely to be an
ultra doubbish sick. This right tail
risk is not adequately positioned for by
investor consensus at the current
juncture. For our June 21st around the
horn, in our view, the Fed's reaction
function will transition from modestly
dobbish to asymmetrically dovish over
the medium to long term due to pressure
from the Trump administration that is
not afraid to challenge the rule of law
or long-standing institutions like Fed
independence. The probability that JP
pal is replaced by an ultra dovish
sycopant is not fully priced and
represents substantial right tail risk
for risk assets short rates and gold and
substantial left tail risk for the US
dollar. Bonds would eventually sell off
prompting intervention like fed yield
curve control but the likely U-shaped
economy punched this risk at least a
couple of quarters into the future.
Elsewhere UK headline CPI accelerated in
June to its highest since January 2024
led by a 4.4% 4% spike in food CBI, the
highest since February 2024. These data
are triggering a backlash over labor's
payroll tax and minimum wage hikes in
April. Rising labor costs from April's
tax and wage hikes have forced
businesses to hike prices, amplifying
inflationary pressure. Recall that both
the US and UK are deep twin deficit
economies that are being run hot from a
fiscal and monetary policy perspective.
Also recall that the US economy is
growing faster than the UK at 2%
year-over-year in Q1 versus 1.3% in the
UK and tracking at 1.7% in Q2 versus
only 1.1% in the UK. With UK real GDP
growth running well below trend, uh this
is a signal that US businesses may also
feel confident in passing along
policyinduced supply chain inflation to
consumers as well. Transition to our 42
macro dashboard. As always, we wrap up
with a question from our community. Uh
this one's titled uh Bitcoin. Uh so um
not really a question but a series of

objectives and also generate uh superior

customizable. You know, we run our
603010 standard case portfolio because

highly effective. Average annual return
on the rolling out of sample back test
since January 2018 is 25% which which is

portfolio. And the maximum draw down is
half of what the 6040 portfolio is with,
you know, 12% versus I want to say 22%.

twice. your portfolio would have crashed
twice since 2018 had you been long 60/40

in January 2018. So super proud of KISS.
